speaker: Alice Chen
text: Hello everyone, and welcome to today's webinar on character consistency in AI-generated video.

speaker: Alice Chen
text: I'm Alice Chen, lead product designer, and I'll be walking you through the best practices we've learned over the past year.

speaker: Alice Chen
text: Joining me today is Bob Martinez, our AI researcher, who'll dive into the technical aspects.

speaker: Bob Martinez
text: Hey everyone, excited to be here.

speaker: Alice Chen
text: So let's start with the core problem. When you're generating a multi-shot sequence, keeping the same character looking consistent across frames is actually really challenging.

speaker: Alice Chen
text: You might notice that in shot one, your character has brown hair, but by shot three, it's suddenly blonde. Or their facial features shift slightly between frames.

speaker: Alice Chen
text: This phenomenon is called identity drift, and it's one of the most common issues users report when working with generative AI.

speaker: Bob Martinez
text: Right. And from a technical standpoint, identity drift happens because each generation is essentially a new inference pass through the model.

speaker: Bob Martinez
text: The model doesn't inherently remember what the character looked like in previous frames. It's working from your text description or reference image each time.

speaker: Alice Chen
text: Exactly. So the safest way to keep a character consistent is to start with a locked reference image before you begin generating your sequence.

speaker: Alice Chen
text: A locked reference means you upload a high-quality image of your character, and the system uses that as the canonical source of truth for all subsequent generations.

speaker: Alice Chen
text: This is way more reliable than relying on text descriptions alone. Text descriptions can be ambiguous, and the model might interpret them differently each time.

speaker: Bob Martinez
text: I want to emphasize this point. If you only use text prompts, you're essentially asking the model to imagine the character from scratch every single time.

speaker: Bob Martinez
text: But with a reference image, you're giving it concrete visual data to anchor on. The difference in consistency is night and day.

speaker: Alice Chen
text: Let me show you a practical example. Here's a sequence where we used only text descriptions. Notice how the character's face shape changes between shots?

speaker: Alice Chen
text: And here's the same sequence, but this time we used a locked reference image. Much more consistent, right?

speaker: Alice Chen
text: Now, let's talk about the workflow. Step one is always to create or select your reference image before you start the main generation process.

speaker: Alice Chen
text: This reference should be high resolution, well-lit, and show the character's face clearly. Avoid references with heavy shadows or extreme angles.

speaker: Bob Martinez
text: One common mistake is waiting until after you've generated a few shots to decide you need a reference. At that point, it's too late to prevent drift.

speaker: Bob Martinez
text: If you try to use one of those already-drifted shots as your reference, you're just locking in the inconsistency.

speaker: Alice Chen
text: Great point. So the workflow is: reference first, then generate. Not the other way around.

speaker: Alice Chen
text: Step two is setting up your project with the reference image locked in place. In our interface, you'll see an option to upload and lock your reference.

speaker: Alice Chen
text: Once it's locked, every frame you generate will use that reference as a guide, keeping your character consistent throughout the sequence.

speaker: Bob Martinez
text: Now, even with a reference image, you might still see minor variations. That's normal. No AI system is perfect.

speaker: Bob Martinez
text: But the variations should be subtle—like slight lighting differences or camera angle effects—not major changes to facial structure or hair color.

speaker: Alice Chen
text: Let's talk about multi-shot sequences specifically. When you're creating a narrative with multiple scenes, planning ahead becomes even more critical.

speaker: Alice Chen
text: We recommend using Brainstorm Mode first to map out your sequence. This helps you think through which shots need character consistency and which don't.

speaker: Alice Chen
text: For example, if your character appears in shots one, three, and five, but not two and four, you only need to worry about consistency for those specific shots.

speaker: Bob Martinez
text: Another tip: generate your character shots in order when possible. Don't jump around. This helps maintain a mental model of the sequence as you work.

speaker: Alice Chen
text: Now, let's address a question we get a lot: What if I want the character to change outfits or be in different lighting between scenes?

speaker: Alice Chen
text: That's totally fine. The reference image locks in facial features and identity, not wardrobe or environment. You can still vary those in your prompts.

speaker: Bob Martinez
text: Exactly. Think of the reference as locking the 'who,' not the 'what' or 'where.' The character's identity stays fixed, but context can change.

speaker: Alice Chen
text: Before we wrap up, let me share some common pitfalls to avoid. Number one: using a group photo as your reference.

speaker: Alice Chen
text: If your reference has multiple people in it, the system might get confused about which face to lock onto. Always use a clear, solo shot.

speaker: Alice Chen
text: Number two: using a heavily stylized or filtered reference. The more natural and true-to-life your reference is, the better the results.

speaker: Bob Martinez
text: Number three: forgetting to check your reference image before you start a big batch generation. Always preview it first.

speaker: Alice Chen
text: And number four: not saving your reference! If you're working on a long-term project, make sure you keep that reference file somewhere safe.

speaker: Alice Chen
text: You might need to come back to this project weeks or months later, and you'll want to use the exact same reference to maintain consistency.

speaker: Bob Martinez
text: One last technical note: our system uses a face recognition model to extract features from your reference. This is different from style transfer.

speaker: Bob Martinez
text: Style transfer would copy the artistic style of an image. Face recognition ensures the actual identity—the person's unique facial structure—remains consistent.

speaker: Alice Chen
text: To summarize: start with a reference, lock it in before generating, use Brainstorm Mode to plan your sequence, and avoid common pitfalls like group photos.

speaker: Alice Chen
text: Follow these guidelines, and you'll see a dramatic improvement in character consistency across your multi-shot projects.

speaker: Bob Martinez
text: We're going to open it up for questions now. Remember, you can always revisit this webinar later if you need a refresher on any of these techniques.

speaker: Alice Chen
text: Thanks for joining us today, everyone. Let's make some consistently great characters!

speaker: Bob Martinez
text: Thanks all, and happy creating!
